Category: Education & Public Sector: Education, Government Services, Non-Profit organizations
Description: When flawed data leads to unequal or delayed support.
Prerequisites: A basic familiarity with how aid organisations operate and a general interest in how technology is used in public services are all that is needed to get started with this course.
Learning objectives: By the end of this course, you will be able to identify common sources of flawed data in humanitarian aid systems and explain how these inaccuracies can lead to unequal or delayed support for vulnerable communities. You will also apply practical strategies to recognise and address data quality issues, helping to ensure that AI tools are used responsibly and fairly within aid distribution programmes.

lessons.Description: In humanitarian crises, aid organisations increasingly rely on artificial intelligence to help decide who receives support, when, and in what quantities. However, when the data feeding these systems is incomplete, biased, or poorly collected, the consequences can be severe, leaving vulnerable people without the help they urgently need. This lesson introduces beginners to the ways in which flawed data can distort AI-driven aid distribution, resulting in unequal or delayed support for affected communities. You will explore real-world examples that illustrate how data quality directly shapes the decisions these systems make, and why understanding this link is essential for anyone working in or alongside the humanitarian sector. By the end of this lesson, you will have a clearer picture of the risks involved and the practical steps organisations can take to improve the reliability of their data before it reaches an AI system.
lessons.content: When flawed data leads to unequal or delayed support.

slides.subtitle: When data shapes who receives help
slides.slidecontent:
• Humanitarian organisations increasingly rely on AI tools to decide who receives food, shelter, and medical aid
• These systems use data to rank needs and allocate resources at scale
• Flawed or incomplete data can quietly exclude the most vulnerable people
• Decisions made by algorithms are not automatically fair or neutral
• Real consequences for real people

slides.subtitle: When missing data means missing people
slides.slidecontent:
• Displaced people often lack official documents, making them invisible to data systems
• No records means no aid
• Informal settlements and remote areas are frequently undercounted or missed entirely
• Language barriers and low literacy can prevent people from registering in the first place
• Even small gaps in data can exclude entire communities from receiving support

slides.subtitle: When past bias shapes current decisions
slides.slidecontent:
• Aid systems trained on incomplete records miss entire communities in need
• Biased historical data reflects past inequalities, not current reality
• Women, displaced persons, and minorities often undercounted in official registries
• Old data excludes new crises and emerging patterns
• Small data gaps compound into large, systemic failures over time

slides.subtitle: How flawed data translates to unequal outcomes
slides.slidecontent:
• Rural areas receive less aid due to underrepresentation in datasets
• Gender gaps skew need assessments, leaving women and girls underserved
• Refugee populations with incomplete records fall through system cracks
• Resources flow to well-documented areas while invisible communities wait
• Biased input produces biased output - the core principle

slides.subtitle: Practical steps to improve data quality
slides.slidecontent:
• Audit data sources regularly to identify gaps and biases
• Include diverse local voices who understand community needs
• Update datasets to reflect current realities, not just historical patterns
• Invest in mobile data collection for hard-to-reach populations
• Document collection methods to understand data limitations

slides.subtitle: Ensuring fair outcomes through ongoing evaluation
slides.slidecontent:
• Test AI systems across different groups before deployment
• Monitor results regularly to catch emerging biases
• Compare AI recommendations with ground-truth community feedback
• Adjust algorithms when certain populations receive consistently poor outcomes
• Build feedback loops from field workers and beneficiaries

slides.subtitle: Stopping bias before it starts
slides.slidecontent:
• Form diverse teams to spot blind spots during system design
• Partner with local organisations who know vulnerable communities
• Use multiple data sources to cross-validate information
• Train staff to recognise signs of data quality problems
• Create transparent processes for questioning AI recommendations

slides.subtitle: Core lessons to carry forward
slides.slidecontent:
• Flawed or biased data in AI systems can directly cause unequal or delayed aid distribution
• Always question the source, completeness, and recency of data feeding any AI tool
• Vulnerable populations are most at risk when data gaps go unchecked
• Small data errors can compound into large, real-world consequences for communities in crisis
• As a next step, audit one data source your organisation currently uses and identify where gaps or biases may exist

scripts.script: Welcome to this course on AI in humanitarian aid distribution. At first glance, using artificial intelligence to help deliver food, shelter, and medical care to people in crisis sounds like a genuinely good idea. These systems can process enormous amounts of data very quickly, helping organisations decide who needs help most urgently and how to allocate limited resources across large populations. But here is where things get complicated. The data these systems rely on is not always complete or accurate, and when it is flawed, the consequences can be serious. People who are already the most vulnerable, those hardest to reach or least visible in the data, can quietly fall through the gaps. And importantly, just because a decision is made by an algorithm does not mean it is fair or neutral. Throughout this course, we will explore how these systems work, where they can go wrong, and what that means for real people in some of the world's most difficult situations.

One of the biggest challenges in humanitarian aid is something that might not be immediately obvious: the data simply isn't complete. When people are displaced, they often don't have official documents, passports, ID cards, or registration papers. And if you don't exist in a system, you can't receive support from it. It's as straightforward, and as troubling, as that. This problem is made worse in informal settlements or hard to reach rural areas, where populations are frequently undercounted or missed altogether. Add to that language barriers and low literacy levels, which can prevent people from registering in the first place, and you start to see how the gaps build up. The really important point here is that these aren't just minor technical oversights. Even a small gap in the data can mean an entire community receives nothing.

When we talk about using AI to distribute aid, one of the biggest problems we face is the quality of the data we feed into these systems. If the records used to train an AI are incomplete or outdated, entire communities can simply be overlooked. And here is the difficult part: a lot of historical data already reflects old inequalities. It was collected in ways that left out women, displaced persons, and minority groups, because they were never properly counted in official registries to begin with. So when an AI learns from that data, it does not learn about current needs, it learns about past blind spots. What makes this especially serious is that even a small gap in the data does not stay small. Over time, those gaps compound, and the result is a system that consistently sends resources to the wrong places while the most vulnerable people receive little or nothing at all.

Now, one of the most serious challenges with AI systems is how flawed data translates into real-world harm. If a system is trained mostly on data from urban areas, rural communities may not receive the support they desperately need. There are also significant gender gaps in how needs are recorded, which means women and girls may not receive appropriate assistance. And refugee populations, who are frequently on the move and difficult to track, often have incomplete records in these systems. The core principle here is simple: biased input produces biased output. The AI can only work with what it is given, so when resources consistently flow to well-documented areas while invisible communities wait, we see the direct consequences of data quality problems.

So what can organisations do to build better data? It starts with regularly auditing your data sources, checking where the information comes from and who might be missing from it. This is where diverse local voices become invaluable - people who understand the communities being served will spot gaps that outsiders simply miss. It's also crucial to keep datasets current, because relying on historical patterns means missing new crises and changing needs. For hard-to-reach populations, investing in mobile data collection can make a real difference. And importantly, document how you collect data, because understanding the limitations of your information is the first step toward improving it.

Building better data is only half the solution - you also need ongoing testing and monitoring to ensure fair outcomes. Before any AI tool is deployed in the field, test how it performs across different groups, not just on average. If one population consistently receives worse outcomes, something needs to change. Once systems are running, regular monitoring is essential because circumstances change and new biases can emerge. Compare AI recommendations with ground-truth feedback from communities themselves, and build feedback loops from field workers who see the results firsthand. This kind of continuous evaluation helps catch problems before they become entrenched.

Prevention is always better than correction when it comes to bias in AI systems. Form diverse teams during the design phase, because people with different backgrounds and lived experiences are far more likely to spot potential problems early. Partner with local organisations who have deep knowledge of vulnerable communities. Use multiple data sources to cross-validate information rather than relying on a single dataset. Train your staff to recognise the warning signs of data quality problems, and create transparent processes that allow people to question and challenge AI recommendations. These prevention strategies help stop bias before it becomes embedded in your systems.

So let's bring together the key lessons from this section. The most important thing to understand is that AI is only as good as the data you feed it. If that data is flawed, incomplete, or biased, the system will reflect those problems, and in humanitarian work, that can mean real people not receiving the help they need. It is worth asking yourself, every time you use an AI tool: where does this data come from, how current is it, and who might be missing from it? The communities most at risk are often the least visible in datasets, and even small gaps can grow into serious consequences when decisions are made at scale. So here is something practical to try before the next lesson. Pick one data source your organisation relies on and take a close look at it. Ask whether it is truly complete, and whether any groups might be underrepresented. That kind of critical thinking is where good AI use begins.

Who is this for: Program managers, aid workers, humanitarian coordinators, nonprofit administrators, government service coordinators, public sector managers, community outreach specialists, social services coordinators
Team or Dept this is for: Humanitarian organizations, Non-profit organizations, Government Services, Public Administration, Social Services, Community Development, International Development
